coredata.dc:title: Assembling all-wood-derived carbon/carbon dots-assisted phase change materials for high-efficiency thermal-energy harvesters
coredata.dc:creator: Shu, Liping
Fang, Heng
Feng, Shichao
Sun, Jianping
Yang, Fang
Hu, Dongying
Cheng, Fangchao
coredata.dc:description: The collection and storage of renewable, sustainable and clean energy including wind, solar, and tidal energy has attracted considerable attention because of its promising potential to replace fossil energy sources. Advanced energy-storage materials are the core component for energy harvesters, affording the high-efficiency conversion of these new-style energy sources. Herein, originated from nature, a series of all-wood-derived carbon-assisted phase change materials (PCMs) were purposed by incorporating carbon dots-modified polyethylene glycol matrix into carbon skeletons via a vacuum-impregnation strategy. The resultant PCMs possessed desired anti-leakage capability and superior thermophysical behaviors. In particular, the optimum sample posed high latent heat (131.5 J/g) and well thermal stability, where the corresponding enthalpy still reserved 90 % over 100 heating/cooling cycles. More importantly, the as-fabricated thermal-energy harvester presented prominent capability to strorage and release multiple forms of thermal energy, as well as high-efficiency solar-energy utilization, corresponding to a photothermal conversion efficiency of 88 % in simulated sunlight irradiation, far exceeding some reported PCMs. Overall, with the introduction of wood-derived carbon dots and carbon skeletons, the assembled all-wood-derived carbon-assisted PCMs afforded trinity advantages on thermal performance, cycling stability, and energy conversion efficiency, which provide a promising potential for the practical application in thermal-energy harvesters.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phase change materials
Wood-derived carbon dots
Carbon skeletons
Photothermal conversion
Thermal-energy harvester

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of wood-derived CDs and carbon skeletons
Fabrication of all-wood-derived carbon-assisted PEG-based composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31960293 ) and the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi ( 2020GXNSFAA297028 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the fabrication of wood-derived CDs and carbon skeletons for assembling PEG-based composites.

The morphologies of x-CDs: TEM images, the insets showed the corresponding digital photos excited with UV light (a), FT-IR spectra (b), XRD patterns (c), and XPS wide-scan spectra (d) and the representative C1s, N1s and O1s core peaks of m-CDs (e).

Schematic of the wood-derived carbon skeletons (a), BET curves of pristine wood and carbon skeletons (b), SEM images of carbon skeletons (c) and PEG-based composites (d), the inset showed the corresponding EDS scanning of N element.

DSC curves of pure PEG and PEG-based composites (a), the thermal conductivity of pure PEG and PEG-based composites (b), the enthalpy of PEG-based composites upon multiple heating/cooling cycles (c), and the anti-leakage capability of pure PEG, CS/PEG, and CS/PEG@m-CDs composites (d).

Schematic of the photothermal conversion mechanism of thermal-energy harvesters assembled by PEG-based composites (a), the temperature-time curves of carbon skeleton and PEG-based composites upon loading/unloading of sunlight irradiation (b-c), and the corresponding photothermal conversion efficiency, as well as compared with other previously reported PCMs (d), and the temperature-time curves of carbon skeleton and PEG-based composites in heating and cooling by a heating plate (e-f).

head.ce:title: Assembling all-wood-derived carbon/carbon dots-assisted phase change materials for high-efficiency thermal-energy harvesters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Featured Metal Materials and Life-cycle Safety for Composite Structures, School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Featured Metal Materials and Life-cycle Safety for Composite Structures
School of Resources, Environment and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Featured Metal Materials and Life-cycle Safety for Composite Structures, School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, College of Chemistry and Materials, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, College of Chemistry and Materials, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The collection and storage of renewable, sustainable and clean energy including wind, solar, and tidal energy has attracted considerable attention because of its promising potential to replace fossil energy sources. Advanced energy-storage materials are the core component for energy harvesters, affording the high-efficiency conversion of these new-style energy sources. Herein, originated from nature, a series of all-wood-derived carbon-assisted phase change materials (PCMs) were purposed by incorporating carbon dots-modified polyethylene glycol matrix into carbon skeletons via a vacuum-impregnation strategy. The resultant PCMs possessed desired anti-leakage capability and superior thermophysical behaviors. In particular, the optimum sample posed high latent heat (131.5 J/g) and well thermal stability, where the corresponding enthalpy still reserved 90 % over 100 heating/cooling cycles. More importantly, the as-fabricated thermal-energy harvester presented prominent capability to strorage and release multiple forms of thermal energy, as well as high-efficiency solar-energy utilization, corresponding to a photothermal conversion efficiency of 88 % in simulated sunlight irradiation, far exceeding some reported PCMs. Overall, with the introduction of wood-derived carbon dots and carbon skeletons, the assembled all-wood-derived carbon-assisted PCMs afforded trinity advantages on thermal performance, cycling stability, and energy conversion efficiency, which provide a promising potential for the practical application in thermal-energy harvesters.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Towards urgent requirements of energy, the development of renewable, sustainable and clean energy such as wind, solar, and tidal energy, has attracted considerable attention because of its promising potential to replace fossil energy sources [1,2]. Nevertheless, the intermittent supply of these new-style energy poses a crucial obstacle for their high-efficiency utilization. In this regard, intelligent energy harvesters have generated widespread interests from scientific vision owing to their tailorable management for the storage and release of collected energy [3–5]. Advanced energy-storage materials are the core component for harvesters, affording the high-efficiency conversion on new-style energy sources [6,7]. Attributed to the high latent heat and superior specific heat capacity, phase change materials (PCMs) as an ideal thermal-energy manager, widely serve for solar collector, chiller plant, thermal controller, etc. [8,9] However, their application is still restricted by some intrinsic deficiencies, e.g., the degeneration of thermophysical performance during multiple cycles and the potential leakage of PCM matrix [10,11]. Focusing on the existing issues, employing appropriate strategies to strengthen thermophysical behaviors and efficiently package PCM matrix is anticipated demands for their applications in thermal-energy harvesters.

Advanced carbon nanomaterials, typically for zero-dimension carbon dots (CDs) [12,13], one-dimension carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [14], and two-dimension graphene (Gr) [15], have been considered as the attractive additives for PCMs so as to enhance their thermophysical performance assisting by the prominent characteristics of carbon nanomaterials, e.g., excellent thermal conductivity [16]. Some works have reported the high-performance PCMs with desired thermophysical capabilities by incorporating carbon nanoparticles into PCM matrix. For instance, Chen et al. reported that novel function of carbon quantum dots (CDs) deriving from acetone and divinyl benzene (DVB) was developed for improving thermal energy harvesting of shape-stabilized composite PCMs [17]. Cao et al. successfully solved the leakage problem and improved the photothermal conversion efficiency using carbon nanotube to absorb paraffin wax [18]. Xu et al. used graphene nanosheets as additive in composite phase change materials to successfully improve the thermal conductivity [19]. Obviously, the successful introduction of carbon nanoparticles could improve the thermophysical properties of PCM-based composites, especially for employing zero-dimension CDs, which pose a low-cost consumption, and simultaneously guarantee the fluidity of PCM matrix in liquid-phase state, thus imparting a positive contribution on the storage and release of collected energy [20]. Nevertheless, the poor interaction between matrix and CDs would lead to the aggregating actions of nanoparticles, resulting in loss of thermal contribution and possible deterioration of processibility of PCM-based composites. To overcome this challenge, various methods have been developed to accelerate the uniform dispersion of carbon nanomaterials into PCM matrix, e.g., surface activation [21], hydrogen bonding [22], and ultrasonic assistance [23], thus taking full advantages of CDs nanoparticles on thermophysical contributions of PCM-based composites.

The synthesis of high-quality CDs is essential to fully contribute to the thermophysical properties of their composites. Compared with conventional carbon sources, wood enriched a large number of carbon-containing molecules (cellulose, hemicelluloses, lignin, etc.) is polymerized and carbonized at appropriate temperature to form the CDs with outstanding advantages, e.g., low cost, renewable, sustainable and environmentally-friendly characteristics, perfectly fitting for the purport of new-style energy harvesters [24,25]. In addition, compared with the single wood-derived carbon source system, combining with organic synthetic compounds possesses more obvious advantages in obtaining high-quality CDs. Phenylenediamine, as a typical aromatic diamine compound, has abundant active amino groups that can provide more remodifying sites in the carbonated reaction, thus synthesizing ideal CDs with the rigid conjugated carbon skeletons based on amino-substituted benzene rings, which would make a positive contribution to the thermophysical capabilities of CDs-incorporated PCM materials [26,27].

Apart from expected thermophysical capabilities, packaging PCMs is a crucial foundation for avoiding the potential leakage of matrix, while occurring solid-liquid phase transition [28]. Generally, traditional packaging templates featuring porous structures could adequately accommodate enough PCM matrix, thus affording ideal energy-storage and release functions, e.g., metal foam [29], aerogel [30] and porous microcapsule [31]. Although the artificial porous materials are successfully responsible for the target goals, the complicated manipulation and high-cost consumption restricted their widespread development and application. Learning from nature, wood-derived porous structures enriched abundant hierarchical pores have become promising candidates for traditional packaging templates, because of the sufficient channels for loading PCM matrix. In addition, the incomparable advantages of natural wood including renewable, sustainable and environmentally-friendly characteristics perfectly fit for the demands of new-style energy harvesters [32]. The problem lies in the fact that the direct utilization of porous wood carrier has low thermal conductivity and low enthalpy value of heat storage and release, which will directly affect the photothermal conversion efficiency of energy collector. Moreover, a brief strategy concerning on the carbonization treatment of pristine wood further imparts plentiful specific surface area to carbon skeletons, ensuring more space for immersing PCM matrix [33]. Besides, the generated carbon skeletons would play a positive contribution on the thermophysical performance of PCMs [34]. Therefore, the successful introduction of as-mentioned CDs and carbon skeletons would give a promising potential for the energy-storage and release of PCM-based composites with desired anti-leakage capability and satisfactory thermophysical performance, especially for the enhanced thermal conductivity. However, there is still a lack of wood-derived CDs and carbon skeletons incorporated into PCM matrix for assembling high-efficiency thermal-energy harvesters to the best of our knowledge.

Herein, in order to fabricate wood-derived CDs and carbon skeletons for assisting the energy-storage and release of PCMs, two separate procedures were proposed to assemble carbon-assisted PCM-based composites in Fig. 1 . On one side, wood-derived CDs were synthesized via a mild hydrothermal reaction by employing pristine wood as carbon sources and three types of phenylenediamine compounds (p-phenylenediamine m-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine) as organic nitrogen sources, and the obtained CDs were signed as p-CDs, m-CDs and o-CDs, respectively. On the other side, pristine wood was carbonized to gain the desired carbon skeletons, affording the sufficient space for immersing polyethylene glycol (PEG) matrix. Followingly, PEG matrix with uniformly dispersed CDs were successfully incorporated into carbon skeletons to assemble the all-wood-derived carbon-assisted PEG-based composites with the assistance of vacuum-impregnation treatment. As expected, the resultant materials possessed desired anti-leakage capability and superior thermophysical behaviors. In particular, the optimum one posed well thermal conductivity and high latent heat, and the corresponding enthalpy still reserved 90 % over 100 heating/cooling cycles. It was simultaneously accompanied with 88 % photothermal conversion efficiency (η), far exceeding some reported PCM composites [25,35]. Overall, with the introduction of CDs and carbon skeletons, the assembled all-wood-derived carbon-assisted PCMs afforded trinity advantages on thermal performance, cycling stability, and energy conversion efficiency, which provide a fundamental guarantee for the promising application in thermal-energy harvesters.

Pristine wood originated from eucalyptus was supported by Nanning Chuangjin plywood Co., Ltd., China. Polyethylene glycol (PEG, molecular weight: 2000) was obtained from Macklin Co., Ltd., China. O-phenylenediamine, m-phenylenediamine, p-phenylenediamine were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd., China. Ultrapure deionized water was provided by Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory, China. All chemicals were used without purification treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of wood-derived CDs and carbon skeletons
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, a part of wood was crushed into chips using a grinder and then sifted out by a 200-meshes screen. Followingly, 3.0 g chips and 0.3 g x-phenylenediamine (x = o, m, p) was added into 30 mL ultrapure deionized water. After stirring intensively, the mixtures were transferred to a 50 mL PPL-lined autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained mixtures were cooled down to room temperature and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 30 min to remove precipitates. The obtained supernatant was refined in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da) for 36 h to gain the target CDs. Therefore, these CDs stored at 4 °C for further experiments. For convenience, the synthesized CDs based on o-phenylenediamine, m-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine were signed as o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs, respectively. On the other side, wood was cut into small blocks (2 × 2 × 0.5 cm3) for preparing carbon skeletons. The as-fabricated blocks were put into a tube furnace for carbonization treatment under N2 environment. Detailedly, the temperature was raised to 260 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C/min for incubating 3 h, and then continuously raised to 1100 °C at 5 °C/min for maintaining another 2 h to prepare the desired carbon skeletons. Before using, the chips and blocks come from pristine wood were completely dried at 100 °C for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of all-wood-derived carbon-assisted PEG-based composites
ce:section.ce:para: The all-wood-derived carbon-assisted PEG-based composites were fabricated via a vacuum-impregnation method. Firstly, three types of synthesized CDs (o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs) (0.2 g) were dispersed separately in 2.0 g PEG under vigorous stirring and ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Then, the uniformly-dispersed PEG-based solution was slowly poured onto the as-prepared carbon skeleton and vacuum-impregnated at 70 °C. In this process, the carbon skeleton needed to continuously flipped to ensure adequate immersion. Briefly, the as-fabricated carbon-assisted PEG-based composites was marked as CS/PEG@o-CD, CS/PEG@m-CD, and CS/PEG@p-CD, respectively. For comparison, the equiponderant PEG was vacuum-impregnated in carbon skeleton based on the same procedures, signed as CS/PEG.

The morphologies of CDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM2100, JEOL, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CDs were recorded by a Fourier spectrometer (Nicolet iS 50, Thermo, USA) in the wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CDs were obtained using a X-ray diffractometer (D/MAX 2500, Rigaku, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CDs was performed by a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectrometer (K-Alpha, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The specific surface area of pristine wood and carbon skeletons were analyzed by a N2 adsorption/desorption analyzer (BK 400, Biobase, China) assisted with Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The cross-section and longitudinal-section morphologies of carbon skeletons and PEG-based composites were observed by a field-emission scanning electron microscope (Sigma 300, Zeiss, Germany) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray (EDS) spectrometer. The differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) curves of pure PEG and PEG-based composites was recorded by a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC214, NETZSCH, Germany). The in-plane thermal conductivity (Tc) of pure PEG and PEG-based composites was determined by a thermal conductivity analyzer (LFA467, NETZSCH, Germany). A light-thermal energy conversion test of carbon skeletons and PEG-based composites was conducted by using a Xenon light source system (CEL-HXF300, EDUCATION AU-LIGHT, China), and the corresponding temperature-time curves were recorded by using a multichannel temperature recorder (RX6000F, Mecon, China). In addition, the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) was calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
η =
mΔH
pS
T t
−
T f
× 100 %
where m is the weight of sample, ΔH is the phase change enthalpy, p is the power density of simulated light source, S is the surface area of sample,
T t
and
T f
are the starting and terminating time during the phase change process, respectively.

Pristine wood featuring renewable, sustainable and environmentally-friendly characteristics, has been treated as green carbon sources for fabricating wood-derived CDs. As a result, the morphologies of obtained CDs were displayed in Fig. 2a. Apparently, the fluorescence behaviors of CDs in aqueous solution under the excitation of ultraviolet (UV) light were predominantly demonstrated the successful synthesis of nano-level CDs (the Inset). Detailedly, in high-resolution TEM images, three types of synthesized CDs performed a crystalline peculiarity with lattice spacings of 0.23, 0.25 and 0.21 nm, which is mainly attributed to the introduction of different nitrogen sources, thus performing various fluorescence characteristics [36]. Notedly, the ordered lattices may be responsible for the lattice plane (100) of graphite carbon, corresponding to the crystallization regions of o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs [37]. In addition, the uniformity of these CDs was evaluated in Fig. S1. The uniform dispersion was noticed at low-resolution TEM images, and the average particle sizes of o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs were calculated, corresponding to 4.25, 5.17 and 3.33 nm, respectively. The differentiated phenomenon was mainly attributed to various nitrogen sources, imparting different surface states or size effects of CDs [38,39]. Thereafter, more detailed structural characteristics of these CDs were explored by XRD, FT-IR, and XPS analyses. As shown in Fig. 2b, there was only a broad diffraction peak at the range of 18 ~ 28o in the XRD patterns, suggesting the existing of amorphous carbon [40]. Moreover, the surface functional groups of CDs were investigated by FT-IR measurements (Fig. 2c). All FT-IR spectra were similar, implying that the same functional groups were enriched on CDs surface, e.g., the stretching vibrations of O − H or N − H groups at 3462 cm−1, CO groups at 1640 cm−1, C − N group at 1418 cm−1, and C − O groups at 1106 cm−1. These characteristic peaks were mainly attributed to a large amount of graphene-enriched sp2 structures in CDs [41], which was also consistent with the results in TEM images. Besides, the XPS spectra of CDs were performed in Fig. 2d-e & Fig. S2. As shown in Fig. 2d, the XPS wide-scan spectrum of CDs exhibited three characteristic peaks at 285.4, 400.6 and 532.3 eV, which were assigned to the C, N and O elements of CDs, respectively, and the detailed atomic percentage of each CDs was also performed in Table S1, where the various proportions endowed CDs with differentiated surface states. What's more, the detailed analyses of C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of one representative sample (m-CDs) were exhibited in Fig. 2e. Three core peaks of C1s spectrum at 284.8, 286.2 and 287.8 eV were observed, assigned to CC, CO and O − C=O bands, respectively. For N1s core peaks, there were two peaks at 399.3 and 400.2 eV, attributing to the C − N − C and N − H bands, and the existing N element implied the successful decoration for CDs with nitrogen source. Noticeably, the high proportion of C − N − C bands in N1s spectrum suggested that the most of N element in CDs existed in the form of pyrrolic-N [42]. The O1s spectrum was divided into three core peaks, locating at 531.3 eV to CO bands, 532.9 eV to C − OH/C − O − C bands, and 533.5 eV to COOH bands, respectively. Similarly, the core peaks of other CDs (o-CDs and p-CDs) performed the same tendency in Fig. S2. Indeed, the wood-derived CDs were successfully synthesized and expected to contribute their thermophysical capability to PEG matrix.

Before assembling all-wood-derived carbon-assisted PCM composites, the wood-derived carbon skeletons as PEG templates were pre-prepared with carbonization process and the schematic was depicted in Fig. 3a. Correspondingly, the pore structures of pristine wood and as-fabricated carbon skeletons were evaluated by N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. 3b). Clearly, two samples posed a typical type-IV N2 isotherm with a distinct hysteresis loop at the range of 0.2–1.0 of relative pressure (P/P0). When P/P0 was below 0.2, the isotherms rose monotonously, indicating that there existed microporous and mesoporous structures, neither in pristine wood or carbonized wood. Nevertheless, the specific surface areas were significantly different between pristine wood and carbon skeletons. The retained carbon skeleton possessed extremely high specific surface area of 785.8 m2·g−1, whereas a low value of 43.6 m2 g−1 was corresponding to pristine wood. This remarkable increase supported ample space for immersing CDs-modified PEG matrix. In addition, the exposed pore morphologies in cross section and longitudinal section of carbon skeletons were observed in Fig. 3c-d. In cross-section direction (Fig. 3c), the uniform honeycomb-like pores were discovered. Meanwhile, the through channels along the extension pores were performed in longitudinal-section direction (Fig. 3d), affording the loading and transport space for PEG matrix. Notedly, the capillary action exiting in these channels would endow the carbon skeletons with strong adhesion, ensuring the efficient immersion of liquid-phase PCM matrix [43,44]. Thereafter, the pure PEG and CDs-modified PEG matrix were programmatically immersed into as-prepared carbon skeletons, signed as CS/PEG and CS/PEG@x-CD (x = o, m, p) samples (Fig. 3e). As comparison, the open channels of PEG-based samples were almost completely filled with matrix in the longitudinal section, suggesting the successful immersion of PEG-based matrix into carbon skeletons. Moreover, the inset of EDS images of N element belonging only to CDs further demonstrated the introduction of CDs-modified PEG matrix into carbon skeletons, ensuring the potential thermophysical contribution to assembled PCMs.

To explore their promising potential in thermal-energy harvesters, the thermophysical properties of pure PEG, CS/PEG and CS/PEG@x-CD (x = o, m, p) composites were investigated in Fig. 4 . Their enthalpy during phase change process were evaluated by DSC measurements (Fig. 4a). Apparently, a slight decline of PEG-based samples was noticed as compared with pure PEG, because there wasn't existing the phase change of incorporated CDs and carbon skeletons. Notedly, the corresponding supercooling temperature was also decreased as bringing in CDs and carbon skeletons, e.g., from 23.3 °C for pure PEG to 18.7 °C for CS/PEG@m-CD sample. This decrease was mainly attributed to the well thermal conductivity (Tc) of carbon skeletons, beneficial for rapid harvesting of thermal energy. Moreover, the corresponding Tc was explored in Fig. 4b & Fig. S3. As expected, the CDs and carbon skeletons paved a positive contribution on PEG matrix, where the Tc of CS/PEG sample reached 0.356 W·m−1 K−1, 69 % higher than pure PEG (0.211 W·m−1 K−1). Meanwhile, after incorporating CDs, the Tc of optimum one was up to 0.454 W·m−1 K−1, realizing 115 % increasement than pure one. The well thermal conduction was benefitted to the cycling stability of PEG-based composites during the phase change process. Hence, the thermal stability of CS/PEG@x-CD (x = o, m, p) samples was verified in Fig. 4c. During multiple heating/cooling cycles, a slight decrease of enthalpy was observed for CS/PEG@x-CD samples. After 100 cycles, the corresponding enthalpy still reserved 83 %, 90 % and 79 % for CS/PEG@o-CD, CS/PEG@m-CD, and CS/PEG@p-CD sample, respectively. Followingly, to further demonstrate the reliability of these PEG-based samples in practical energy-storage application, the anti-leakage capability of all samples was compared in Fig. 4d and Fig. S4. Typically, the solid-phase PEG (CS/PEG) started to melt after maintaining 5 min at 70 °C, and then flowed until completely wetting substrates. On the contrary, the other PEG-based samples retained solid-phase features over time, exhibiting the excellent anti-leakage capability of PEG-based composites, because of the strong capillary action of carbon skeletons onto PEG matrix.

The well thermophysical performance of all-wood-derived carbon-assisted PEG-based composites has been fully demonstrated in former. Followingly, their promising potential in thermal-energy harvesters was evaluated in Fig. 5 . A schematic regarding on the operational mechanism of photothermal conversion including the energy storage and release of thermal-energy harvesters assembled by PEG-based materials was depicted in Fig. 5a. Under the sunlight irradiation, the harvester absorbs solar energy and stimulates electrons to convert heat, simultaneously accompanied by temperature-rise period. While the temperature reaches the melting point of PCMs, heat energy was storage as latent heat in the system. When the surrounding temperature is lower than the harvester, the system would spontaneously release the stored energy [45]. As a proof-of-concept, the energy-storage and release system was constructed by employing as-fabricated PEG-based materials. The temperature-time curves were recorded in simulating the loading/unloading of sunlight irradiation (Fig. 5b-c). No matter in temperature-rise (loading sunlight irradiation) or temperature-decline (unloading sunlight irradiation) process, there was a temperature plateau for all PEG-based samples, whereas the temperature of carbon skeletons changed monotonously, which was due to the effect of immersed PEG matrix for efficiently storing and releasing thermal energy. Moreover, the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) during sunlight-irradiation process was calculated in Fig. 5d. Remarkably, the incorporation of CDs into PEG matrix played a positive contribution on the η, where the η value was up to 83 %, 88 % and 81 %, for CS/PEG@o-CD, CS/PEG@m-CD, and CS/PEG@p-CD sample, respectively, as the η value of CS/PEG sample was only 59 %. The different behavior was attributed to the well thermophysical properties including lower supercooling temperature and high Tc of CDs-modified PEG matrix. More importantly, the assembled PEG-based composites possessed prominent superiority in η as compared with other reported PCMs [20,22,25,35,46–48]. Apart from photothermal conversion, harvesting for other forms of thermal energy were equally essential for the assembled energy harvesters. Thus, the temperature-time curves of all PEG-based samples were marked in heating and cooling by a heating plate (Fig. 5e-f). Obviously, the temperature plateau appeared in the heating and cooling process, indicating that these harvesters presented capability to strorage and release multiple forms of thermal energy, e.g., as-mentioned solar energy and waste heat energy. Overall, with the experimental evaluations, our designed all-wood-derived carbon-assisted PCMs afforded trinity advantages on thermal performance, cycling stability, and energy conversion efficiency, which pose the promising potential for the practical application in thermal-energy harvesters.

In summary, with the assistance of vacuum-impregnation strategy, a series of all-wood-derived carbon-assisted PCMs signed as CS/x-CD/PEG (x = o, m, p) were prepared by incorporating CDs-modified PEG matrix into carbon skeletons. Detailedly, three types of CDs (o-CDs, m-CDs and p-CDs) were synthesized by a mild hydrothermal reaction, and then compounded with PEG matrix, and finally immersed them into the as-fabricated carbon skeletons to obtained carbon-assisted PCMs featuring excellent anti-leakage capability and desired thermophysical performance. In particular, the CS/x-CD/PEG (x = o, m, p) materials presented high latent heat and well thermal stability, where the corresponding enthalpy still reserved 83 %, 90 % and 79 % respectively, over 100 heating/cooling cycles. Notedly, the assembled thermal-energy harvester with CS/x-CD/PEG materials posed outstanding capability to strorage and release multiple forms of thermal energy, exhibiting high-efficiency solar-energy utilization, and their photothermal conversion efficiency in simulated sunlight irradiation was up to 83 %, 88 % and 81 % respectively, significantly higher than that of the comparison, corresponding to 59 % photothermal conversion efficiency. Therefore, with the introduction of wood-derived CDs and carbon skeletons, the assembled all-wood-derived carbon-assisted PCMs afforded promising advantages in their potential application of thermal-energy harvesters.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
Liping Shu: Writing−Original Draft
Heng Fang: Investigation, Methodology
Shichao Feng: Methodology
Jianping Sun: Conceptualization
Fang Yang: Conceptualization
Dongying Hu*: Supervision, Review and Editing
Fangchao Cheng*: Supervision, Review and Editing

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31960293) and the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (2020GXNSFAA297028).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.128365.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sunlight-coordinated high-performance moisture power in natural conditions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Bai, Y. Huang, H. Wang, T. Guang, Q. Liao, H. Cheng, S. Deng, Q. Li, Z. Shuai, L. Qu, Sunlight-coordinated high-performance moisture power in natural conditions, advanced materials 34(10) (2022) 2103897, doi:10.1002/adma.202103897.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainable thermal energy batteries from fully bio-based transparent wood

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bio-physicochemical dual energy harvesting fabrics for self-sustainable smart electronic suits
